purpose: State-wise investment IMPLEMENTATION combining domestic and foreign capital, built on IEM Part B (the filing an industrial undertaking makes when commercial production actually commences) -- the one official dataset that measures implemented industrial investment regardless of who funded it. Ranks states 2021-2025, computes intent-to-implementation conversion per state, contrasts against FY2025-26 FDI equity to infer each state's financing mix, and identifies the sector leaders driving implementation.
method: DPIIT Annual Report 2025-26, Appendices III (Part A state-wise), V (Part B state-wise) and VI (Part B sector-wise), Grand-Total-verified layout extraction from the source PDF. FDI comparison column from DPIIT FDI Newsletter XXXIV-4 Table 13 (FY2025-26 state-wise equity inflow). The property that made IEM unusable as an FDI proxy elsewhere in this repo -- it mixes domestic and foreign capital indiscriminately -- is precisely what makes it the right combined measure here.
what_iem_part_b_measures: Implemented industrial projects under the I(D&R) Act 1951 -- manufacturing, not services (Karnataka's software economy is invisible here by design).
Only filings above the eligibility threshold (Rs 50cr plant & machinery, raised to Rs 125cr from Apr 2025) -- large projects only.
Capital source is NOT recorded: a Tata plant and a Daikin plant file identically. Domestic + foreign combined.
The 1951 taxonomy has no Electronics category -- electronics investment appears under Electricals Equipment / Comm-Office Equipment / Telecommunications.

state_table_part_b_rs_cr.financing_read: Both engines at scale -- and implementing 178% of same-period intent (the pre-2021 pipeline landing). Rs 4.55 lakh cr in 2024-2025 alone.
THE domestic-capital state, quantified: #2 in implementation with 0.14% of national FDI. Tata Steel/Jindal steel capex is here -- invisible to FDI data, enormous in IEM data.
#3 implementation AND #1 FDI ($18.4bn, 31.3%) -- the most FDI-weighted major manufacturing state (foreign OEM auto belt), though conversion of its huge stated intent is just 35%.
Domestic-led manufacturing with selective genuine FDI (Sri City: Daikin, LG); 85% conversion and a Rs 50,680cr 2025 -- accelerating.
The 356% conversion is a small-base artifact (2023's Rs 32,182cr spike vs tiny same-period intent) -- old filings landing, not a boom in new intent.
Surprisingly modest in IEM terms for the 'Detroit of Asia' -- its auto cluster largely predates this 5-year window; FDI ($4.7bn) outweighs new large-project IEM implementation.

THE inversion: 2.2% of national implementation but 22% of national FDI ($12.9bn). Its FDI is services/software (invisible to IEM by design); its manufacturing implementation is modest and its conversion (21%) the weakest of the majors.
Modest implementation so far -- but 2025 Part A intent spiked to Rs 60,991cr (life-sciences push), so watch the B column from 2026.
The 2023 Rs 18,778cr is the Tata Electronics-era surge registering in IEM -- while FDI shows $2.37M. The clearest single-state proof that IEM captures what FDI data structurally cannot.
Steady small-scale implementation despite the 2025 incentive revocation.

grand_total_note: All-India Part B 2021-2025: Rs 19,51,828cr across 4,859 implemented IEMs. The top 3 states (Gujarat, Odisha, Maharashtra) account for 63.3% of all implemented industrial investment.

sector_leaders_part_b_rs_cr.read: The implementation engine of Indian industry -- Rs 2.09 lakh cr in 2025 alone (the largest single-year sector figure in the series). This is the Odisha/Gujarat/Chhattisgarh steel-and-metals capex, overwhelmingly domestic-funded (the same sector whose FDI proxy is amber and whose PLI has disbursed 0.8%).

The quiet giant: #2 implementation sector WITH NO PLI SCHEME. Private chemical capex is landing at Rs 50-80,000cr/yr regardless -- strengthening the case that the proposed chemicals PLI would be pushing an already-moving door (and sharpening the question of why imports still grew 86%: the capex may be import-substituting slowly, or demand is simply outrunning it).
Where electronics/white-goods manufacturing lands in the 1951 taxonomy -- steady Rs 19-69,000cr/yr.
ANOMALY FLAG: Rs 1,22,497cr of the 5-yr total sits in a single year (2024) against Rs 1-3,000cr in every other year -- either one mega-project (solar glass?) or a data quirk. Not treated as a trend.
Consistent riser (Rs 7,236cr in 2021 to Rs 36,888cr in 2024) -- matches Food Processing PLI being one of the few schemes paying out at scale.
ANOMALY FLAG: Rs 70,117cr of the total is one year (2025) -- likely a single refinery/energy mega-filing.
The auto/EV capex channel -- Rs 19,138cr in 2025, rising.
2025 riser (Rs 23,854cr vs Rs 5-10,000cr/yr prior) -- large textile projects finally implementing even as the PLI's own disbursal sits at 0.5%.
2025 riser (Rs 18,077cr vs Rs 3,599cr in 2021 -- a 5x climb) that lines up with the sector's FDI doubling and its A-grade PLI: pharma is the sector where every measure now points the same way.

iem_vs_fdi_divergence.states: Odisha (17.2% of implementation, 0.14% of FDI), Assam (Rs 18,778cr in 2023 vs $2.37M FDI), Chhattisgarh, Rajasthan, MP
iem_vs_fdi_divergence.meaning: These states' industrialization is real and large -- and structurally invisible to anyone reading only FDI tables.

iem_vs_fdi_divergence.pattern: High FDI, modest implementation (services FDI or pre-window clusters)
iem_vs_fdi_divergence.states: Karnataka (22% of FDI, 2.2% of implementation -- Bengaluru software), Tamil Nadu (FDI $4.7bn vs 2.3% implementation share -- auto cluster predates the window)
iem_vs_fdi_divergence.meaning: Their headline FDI is not new large-scale manufacturing; IEM strips the services/HQ effect out.

iem_vs_fdi_divergence.states: Gujarat (#1 implementation at 33.8% AND #4 FDI), Maharashtra (#3 implementation AND #1 FDI), Andhra Pradesh (85% conversion + genuine Sri City FDI)
iem_vs_fdi_divergence.meaning: The states that win both channels -- and Gujarat's 178% conversion means it implements more than it files intent for, the sign of a deep pre-existing pipeline.

caveats: A-to-B conversion is a same-period flow ratio, not cohort-matched -- Part B in any year comes from Part A filings of earlier years (Punjab's 356% and Gujarat's 178% are pipeline effects, not same-year magic).

DPIIT publishes state-wise and sector-wise Part B as separate marginals -- NO state-x-sector cross-tab exists, so 'which sector leads in which state' cannot be answered from this data and is inferred only where external evidence exists (e.g. Odisha=metals).

Appendix V is headed 'Up to Dec 2025' while Appendix VI says 'Up to Nov 2025' -- yet both grand-total to the identical Rs 5,83,096cr; flagged as an internal labeling inconsistency in DPIIT's own document.
2025 is a partial year and IEM partial-year figures revise heavily (2024's Part B grew Rs ~2 lakh cr between consecutive Annual Reports).
IEM excludes services, projects below the threshold, and industries still under compulsory licensing.
